title: Security Fundamentals
summary: OWASP Top 10, SQL injection, XSS, CSRF, JWT, OAuth 2.0, and secure coding practices every developer must know.
guide:
# Security Fundamentals ## Why Security Matters (Even for Backend Devs) In 2021, a Log4Shell vulnerability in a Java logging library allowed attackers to run arbitrary code on millions of servers worldwide. The fix needed one line — but developers who didn't understand the attack couldn't recognise it fast enough. Security is not a separate team's job. Every developer writes code that touches user data, handles authentication, and processes untrusted input. Understanding the most common attacks is the difference between shipping safe software and shipping a ticking bomb. ``` Attacker mindset vs Developer mindset: Developer: 'The user enters their email in this form field.' Attacker: 'What if I enter: admin@site.com\'-- ?' (SQL injection that bypasses login!) Developer: 'We store passwords in the database.' Attacker: 'Are they hashed? Which algorithm? Can I reverse them?' Security = thinking like an attacker while building like a developer. ``` --- ## OWASP Top 10 — The Most Common Web Vulnerabilities OWASP (Open Web Application Security Project) publishes a list of the top 10 most critical web security risks. Every developer should know all 10. ``` +----+--------------------------------+------------------------------------------+ | # | Vulnerability | One-line description | +----+--------------------------------+------------------------------------------+ | A1 | Broken Access Control | Users doing things they shouldn't | | A2 | Cryptographic Failures | Weak/missing encryption of sensitive data| | A3 | Injection (SQL, NoSQL, OS) | Untrusted data interpreted as commands | | A4 | Insecure Design | Missing threat modelling, bad patterns | | A5 | Security Misconfiguration | Default passwords, open cloud buckets | | A6 | Vulnerable Components | Old libraries with known CVEs | | A7 | Auth Failures | Weak passwords, session fixation | | A8 | Software & Data Integrity | Unsigned updates, compromised CI/CD | | A9 | Logging & Monitoring Failures | Can't detect or respond to breaches | | A10| SSRF | Server makes requests to internal systems| +----+--------------------------------+------------------------------------------+ ``` --- ## SQL Injection — The Classic Attack SQL injection happens when user input is concatenated directly into a SQL query. ``` VULNERABLE CODE (Java): String query = "SELECT * FROM users WHERE email='" + email + "' AND password='" + pass + "'"; Attacker enters email: admin@site.com'-- Resulting query: SELECT * FROM users WHERE email='admin@site.com'--' AND password='...' -- is a SQL comment. Everything after it is IGNORED. Attack logs in as admin without the password! Even worse — attacker enters: ' OR '1'='1 SELECT * FROM users WHERE email='' OR '1'='1' AND password='' '1'='1' is ALWAYS true. Returns ALL users. Attacker picks first (usually admin). FIX — Parameterised queries / Prepared statements: // Java PreparedStatement stmt = conn.prepareStatement( "SELECT * FROM users WHERE email=? AND password=?"); stmt.setString(1, email); stmt.setString(2, password); // Parameters are NEVER interpreted as SQL — they're data only. // Spring Data JPA — automatic parameterization: @Query("SELECT u FROM User u WHERE u.email = :email") Optional findByEmail(@Param("email") String email); ``` **Rule: NEVER concatenate user input into SQL. Always use parameterised queries.** --- ## XSS — Cross-Site Scripting XSS happens when your app renders untrusted user content as HTML — allowing attackers to inject JavaScript that runs in other users' browsers. ``` SCENARIO: Comments section Attacker posts comment: Vulnerable app renders it as HTML:
Every other user who views this comment gets their session cookie stolen! Attacker can now log in AS those users. Types: Stored XSS: Malicious script saved in DB, served to all visitors (worst) Reflected XSS: Script in URL parameter, reflected back in response DOM XSS: Client-side code writes attacker data to DOM without sanitisation FIXES: 1. Escape output — convert < to <, > to > before rendering 2. React/Angular escape by default (use dangerouslySetInnerHTML/[innerHTML] carefully!) 3. Content Security Policy header — browser refuses to run inline scripts 4. HttpOnly cookies — JS can't read them even if XSS succeeds CSP header example: Content-Security-Policy: default-src 'self'; script-src 'self' 'nonce-abc123' // Only scripts from same origin OR with matching nonce run ``` --- ## CSRF — Cross-Site Request Forgery ``` SCENARIO: You are logged into your bank. bank.com sets a session cookie. While still logged in, you visit evil.com which has: Your browser sees an img tag, tries to load the URL. It automatically includes your bank.com session cookie. Bank receives the request — it looks legitimate (valid session cookie). Money transferred to attacker. You did nothing wrong. FIXES: 1. CSRF tokens — unique random token per form, verified server-side POST /transfer sends this token; server validates it matches session 2. SameSite cookies (modern fix): Set-Cookie: sessionId=abc; SameSite=Strict // Cookie NOT sent on cross-site requests. CSRF impossible. SameSite=Lax (default): sent on top-level navigation (links), not sub-requests SameSite=Strict: never sent cross-site 3. Check Origin/Referer header — reject requests from unexpected origins Spring Security adds CSRF protection automatically. Disable only for stateless APIs that use JWT (not cookies). ``` --- ## JWT — JSON Web Tokens ``` JWT is a compact, self-contained token for stateless authentication. Structure (3 base64-encoded parts separated by dots): header.payload.signature Example (decoded): Header: { alg: 'HS256', typ: 'JWT' } Payload: { sub: 'user123', role: 'admin', exp: 1714000000, iat: 1713990000 } Signature: HMACSHA256(base64(header) + '.' + base64(payload), secretKey) How it works: 1. User logs in with email/password 2. Server verifies credentials, creates JWT signed with secret key 3. JWT returned to client (stored in memory or HttpOnly cookie) 4. Every subsequent request includes JWT in Authorization header: Authorization: Bearer eyJhbGciOiJIUzI1NiJ9... 5. Server verifies signature — no DB lookup needed (stateless!) Verify means: Server recomputes signature with its secret key. If it matches the signature in the token: token is authentic. If someone tampers with the payload (changes role to 'admin'): signature fails. CRITICAL RULES: - NEVER store JWT in localStorage (XSS can steal it) - Store in HttpOnly cookie (JS cannot access it) - Set short expiry (15 min access token) + refresh token (7 days) - Use asymmetric keys (RS256) so services can verify without sharing secret - Validate exp, iat, iss, aud claims JWT vs Session: Session: Stateful — server stores session, checks DB on every request JWT: Stateless — token is self-contained, no DB lookup needed JWT scales: many servers can verify without shared session store JWT risk: Can't invalidate before expiry (logout doesn't destroy token) Mitigate with short expiry + refresh token rotation ``` --- ## OAuth 2.0 — Delegated Authorization ``` PROBLEM: You use a 3rd party app (Trello) and want it to read your Google Calendar. BAD approach: give Trello your Google password (now Trello has your password!) GOOD approach: OAuth 2.0 OAuth 2.0 flow (Authorization Code): 1. Trello redirects you to Google: https://accounts.google.com/o/oauth2/v2/auth ?client_id=trello-client-id &redirect_uri=https://trello.com/callback &scope=calendar.read &response_type=code &state=random-csrf-token 2. You log in to Google and consent to 'Trello wants to read Calendar' 3. Google redirects back to Trello with a short-lived authorization CODE: https://trello.com/callback?code=4/abc123&state=random-csrf-token 4. Trello's backend exchanges code for access_token (secret exchange, server-to-server): POST https://oauth2.googleapis.com/token { code: '4/abc123', client_id: ..., client_secret: ..., grant_type: 'authorization_code' } Response: { access_token: 'ya29...', expires_in: 3600, refresh_token: '...' } 5. Trello uses access_token to call Google Calendar API on your behalf Key points: - Trello NEVER sees your Google password - Access token is scoped (only calendar.read, not gmail, drive) - Access token expires (usually 1 hour) - Refresh token gets new access tokens without you re-logging in ``` --- ## Password Storage ``` WRONG (never do): Store plain text password MD5/SHA1 hash (fast = crackable) RIGHT: bcrypt / Argon2 / scrypt Why bcrypt? bcrypt is INTENTIONALLY SLOW. Each check takes ~100ms. For users: 100ms is imperceptible. For attackers brute-forcing: 100ms per guess = 10 guesses/second. SHA1: billions of guesses per second on a GPU. bcrypt: 10,000 guesses per second. Makes brute force 100,000x harder. bcrypt hash includes the salt automatically: $2a$12$N9qo8uLOickgx2ZMRZoMyeIjZAgcfl7p92ldGxad68LJZdL17lhWy ^^ ^^ ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ alg cost salt + hash (combined) cost 12 = 2^12 = 4096 rounds Java Spring Security: PasswordEncoder encoder = new BCryptPasswordEncoder(12); String hash = encoder.encode(rawPassword); boolean match = encoder.matches(rawPassword, storedHash); ``` --- ## HTTPS and TLS ``` HTTP: Plain text. Anything between you and server can read it. HTTPS: Encrypted using TLS (Transport Layer Security). TLS Handshake (simplified): 1. Client: 'Hello, I support TLS 1.3, here are my cipher suites' 2. Server: 'I choose AES-256. Here is my certificate (public key).' 3. Client: verifies certificate against trusted CAs 4. Client: generates random session key, encrypts with server's public key 5. Server: decrypts with private key. Now both have same session key. 6. All subsequent communication encrypted with symmetric session key. Certificate: proves you're really talking to bank.com (not an impersonator) Signed by a trusted Certificate Authority (Let's Encrypt, DigiCert) Browser has list of trusted CAs built in HTTPS requirements for developers: - Always HTTPS in production (never HTTP for sensitive data) - HSTS header: Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains (tells browser: ONLY use HTTPS for this domain for 1 year) - Redirect HTTP to HTTPS at load balancer/nginx - Don't log sensitive data (passwords, tokens, card numbers) ``` --- ## Security Headers Cheatsheet ``` Header Purpose ────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────────── Content-Security-Policy Control which scripts/styles/images load Strict-Transport-Security Force HTTPS for N seconds X-Frame-Options: DENY Prevent clickjacking (embedding in iframe) X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff Browser must use declared content-type Referrer-Policy: no-referrer Don't send full URL as referer Permissions-Policy Disable camera/mic/geolocation ``` --- ## Mind Map ``` SECURITY FUNDAMENTALS | +-- OWASP TOP 10 | +-- A1: Broken Access Control | +-- A3: Injection (SQL, XSS) | +-- A7: Auth Failures | +-- INJECTION ATTACKS | +-- SQL Injection -> parameterised queries | +-- XSS -> escape output, CSP, HttpOnly | +-- CSRF -> SameSite cookies, CSRF tokens | +-- AUTHENTICATION | +-- JWT (stateless) vs Session (stateful) | +-- OAuth 2.0 (delegated auth) | +-- bcrypt password hashing | +-- TRANSPORT | +-- HTTPS / TLS | +-- HSTS | +-- Security headers | +-- SECURE CODING RULES +-- Never trust user input +-- Parameterise all queries +-- HttpOnly + SameSite cookies +-- Short-lived tokens +-- Principle of least privilege ``` --- ## How Security Connects to Other Topics - **Docker/K8s**: Run containers as non-root. Security contexts, secrets management. - **AWS**: IAM least privilege, S3 bucket policies, VPC security groups, KMS encryption. - **CI/CD**: Pipeline secrets must not be in code. Secret scanning, SAST tools. - **APIs/REST**: Always validate input, authenticate all endpoints, rate-limit. - **Databases**: Parameterised queries, encryption at rest, minimal DB user privileges. --- ## References and Further Learning ### Videos - **Web Security in 100 Seconds + Full Course** by Fireship: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=iXSCFuXMgjg - **OWASP Top 10 Explained** by David Bombal: https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=YqoxSzDzIG8 ### Articles - **OWASP Top 10**: https://owasp.org/www-project-top-ten/ - **PortSwigger Web Security Academy** (free, hands-on labs): https://portswigger.net/web-security - **JWT.io** — decode and inspect JWTs: https://jwt.io ### Practice - **DVWA** (Deliberately Vulnerable Web App): https://dvwa.co.uk/ — practice SQL injection, XSS safely - **PortSwigger labs** — browser-based labs, no setup needed

questions.prompt: Which OWASP Top 10 category covers SQL injection, XSS, and OS command injection?
questions.explanation: Injection (A3) covers all cases where untrusted data is sent to an interpreter as part of a command or query — SQL, NoSQL, OS commands, LDAP, XSS.

questions.prompt: A login form query is: SELECT * FROM users WHERE email='<input>'. Attacker enters email: admin'--. What happens?
choices: SQL comment (--) ignores the password check, logging in as admin with no password
questions.explanation: -- is a SQL comment. Everything after it is ignored. The query becomes: SELECT * FROM users WHERE email='admin' — password check bypassed. Fix: use parameterised queries.

questions.prompt: What is the correct fix for SQL injection?
choices: Use parameterised queries / prepared statements — user input is never interpreted as SQL
questions.explanation: Parameterised queries separate code from data. The parameter placeholder (?) is never treated as SQL — it's always data. This fundamentally prevents SQL injection regardless of input content.

questions.prompt: Stored XSS differs from Reflected XSS because:
choices: Stored XSS is saved in the database and served to ALL future visitors; Reflected XSS is in the URL and affects only users who click the link
questions.explanation: Stored XSS persists — one injection poisons all visitors. Reflected XSS needs the victim to click a crafted URL. Both are serious but stored XSS has higher blast radius.

questions.prompt: Why should session cookies use HttpOnly flag?
choices: Prevents JavaScript from reading the cookie — even if XSS succeeds, attacker JS cannot steal the session token
HttpOnly makes it work on HTTP
questions.explanation: HttpOnly tells the browser: 'This cookie is for HTTP requests only, not accessible via document.cookie.' If XSS injects script, it cannot read the session cookie. This breaks the most common XSS attack pattern.

questions.prompt: What is CSRF and what does SameSite=Strict cookie attribute do?
choices: CSRF is Cross-Site Scripting; SameSite blocks all cookies

CSRF is Cross-Site Request Forgery — tricking your browser into making requests to another site using your existing cookies. SameSite=Strict prevents the browser from sending cookies on any cross-site request.
CSRF is a clickjacking attack; SameSite prevents iframes
CSRF is a SQL attack; SameSite has no relation
questions.explanation: CSRF exploits that browsers automatically attach cookies to all requests. SameSite=Strict tells the browser: only send this cookie when the request originates from the same site. Cross-site requests (the attacker's page) get no cookie. CSRF impossible.

questions.prompt: A JWT payload contains { role: 'user' }. Attacker changes it to { role: 'admin' } and sends the modified token. What happens?
choices: Server accepts it — role is now admin
Server rejects it — signature verification fails because payload was tampered
Server logs a warning but proceeds
questions.explanation: JWT signature covers the entire header + payload. Any change to the payload invalidates the signature. When the server re-computes the expected signature and compares, the mismatch reveals tampering. Token rejected.

questions.prompt: Why is storing JWT in localStorage dangerous?
choices: Any JavaScript on the page (including injected XSS scripts) can read localStorage — token stolen via document.cookie-equivalent
questions.explanation: localStorage is accessible by any JS on the page. XSS attack: inject one script that reads localStorage and sends it to evil.com. Token stolen. Use HttpOnly cookie — JS cannot access it.

questions.prompt: OAuth 2.0 Authorization Code flow: what does the 'code' returned to the redirect_uri represent?
choices: A short-lived one-time code that the app's backend exchanges server-to-server for the real access token
questions.explanation: The code is a short-lived (10 seconds typically), one-use authorization code. The backend exchanges it privately (with client_secret) for the access token. The access token never appears in the browser URL. This protects it from browser history and logs.

questions.prompt: Why does bcrypt include a salt in the stored hash?
choices: A random salt means identical passwords produce different hashes — prevents rainbow table attacks and shows if two users have same password
questions.explanation: Without salt: everyone with password 'abc123' has the same hash. An attacker with a rainbow table (precomputed hash→password mapping) cracks all 'abc123' users at once. Salt makes every hash unique — rainbow tables useless.

questions.prompt: What is the Principle of Least Privilege?
choices: Use the minimum version of software
Every user/service/process should have only the permissions it actually needs to function — nothing extra
Least privilege only applies to databases
questions.explanation: If your payment service only needs to read/write the orders table, don't give it full DB admin. If compromised, the blast radius is limited to what it had access to. Apply to: IAM roles, DB users, service accounts, OS processes.

questions.prompt: What is the purpose of the Content-Security-Policy header?
choices: Limits where browser can load scripts, styles, images from — prevents XSS by blocking execution of injected scripts from external or inline sources
questions.explanation: CSP is a browser-enforced whitelist of trusted content sources. Even if XSS injects an evil script, CSP prevents it from loading/running if it doesn't match the policy. A strong CSP is the strongest XSS mitigation.

questions.prompt: Which security practices protect against SQL injection? (Select all)
questions.explanation: Parameterised queries are the primary fix. Input validation (whitelist expected formats) adds defence-in-depth. ORMs use parameterised queries internally. Disabling DB is not practical.

questions.prompt: What does HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security) header do?
choices: Tells the browser: only access this domain over HTTPS for the specified duration — prevents SSL stripping attacks
questions.explanation: HSTS prevents attackers from downgrading HTTPS to HTTP (SSL stripping). Browser receives max-age=31536000 and refuses to connect via HTTP for 1 year. Even if user types http://, browser auto-upgrades.

questions.prompt: Broken Access Control (OWASP A1) example: user with role 'viewer' calls DELETE /users/42. Server deletes user. What was missing?
choices: Server-side authorisation check — server trusted client-side role without verifying on the server the caller actually has delete permission
questions.explanation: Never trust client-side role/permission claims. Verify authorisation server-side on every request: 'Does THIS authenticated user have DELETE permission?' RBAC/ABAC enforced at backend, not just hidden UI buttons.

questions.prompt: Why is MD5 unsuitable for password hashing in 2025?
choices: MD5 is fast — GPUs can compute billions of MD5 hashes per second, making brute force and rainbow table attacks trivial
MD5 is not available in Java
MD5 only works for file checksums
questions.explanation: Speed is the enemy of password hashing. bcrypt/Argon2's intentional slowness (100ms+) makes brute force 10 million times harder than fast hashes. MD5's speed is a security liability for passwords.

questions.prompt: What is a refresh token in JWT-based auth? Why is it useful?
choices: A long-lived token stored securely that can exchange for new short-lived access tokens without re-login — allows short access token expiry (15 min) without annoying users
A token that refreshes the page
questions.explanation: Access tokens short expiry (15 min) limits damage if stolen. Refresh tokens (7 days, stored in HttpOnly cookie) silently get new access tokens. When refresh token expires, user must log in again. Rotation: each refresh issues a new refresh token and invalidates old one.

questions.prompt: SSRF (Server-Side Request Forgery) attack: attacker sends { url: 'http://169.254.169.254/latest/meta-data/' }. What is the risk?
choices: The server fetches the URL — 169.254.169.254 is the AWS metadata endpoint that returns IAM credentials. Attacker gets your cloud credentials.
questions.explanation: If your app fetches user-supplied URLs, attackers can target internal services: AWS metadata (169.254.169.254), internal APIs, Redis on localhost:6379. Fix: validate/whitelist URLs, block internal IP ranges, use VPC firewall rules.

questions.prompt: A user uploads an image. Your server saves it to /uploads/. Filename was: '../../../etc/passwd'. What is this attack?
choices: Path traversal — attacker uses ../ to escape the intended directory and overwrite/read sensitive files
questions.explanation: Path traversal (directory traversal) uses ../ sequences in filenames/paths to escape the intended directory. Fix: normalise and sanitise paths, use Path.resolve() and verify it starts with the expected base directory, use random UUIDs as filenames.

questions.prompt: Why is it dangerous to log user passwords, even in error logs?
choices: Logs are often stored in plain text, accessible to many engineers and log aggregation services — password in a log = immediate breach of that account, and users reuse passwords
questions.explanation: Logs are accessed by many people and systems. A password in a log survives for months. Users reuse passwords — breach one, breach many. Never log: passwords, tokens, private keys, card numbers, SSNs. Mask PII in logs.

flashcards.back: Use parameterised queries / prepared statements. Input is NEVER interpreted as SQL — only as data. PreparedStatement in Java, @Query with @Param in Spring, ORM automatically parameterises.

1. Escape output (< → <) before rendering. 2. HttpOnly cookies (JS can't steal session). 3. Content-Security-Policy header (browser blocks injected scripts). 4. React/Angular escape by default — never use dangerouslySetInnerHTML with user input.

Set-Cookie: session=abc; SameSite=Strict. Browser will NOT send this cookie on cross-site requests. Eliminates CSRF for modern browsers. For older: add CSRF token (random value in form, verified server-side).
HttpOnly cookie: JS cannot read it. Safe from XSS. localStorage: readable by any JS. Vulnerable to XSS. Use HttpOnly + SameSite=Strict cookie. Short access token (15 min) + longer refresh token.

1. Redirect to auth server with scope. 2. User consents. 3. Auth server returns short-lived code. 4. Backend exchanges code + client_secret for access_token (server-to-server). Access token never in URL.

Each +1 to cost doubles computation time. Cost 12 = 4096 rounds, ~100ms. Choose cost so hashing takes 100-300ms on your server. Re-evaluate yearly as hardware gets faster. Never MD5/SHA1 for passwords.

A1: Broken Access Control (verify authorisation server-side every request). A3: Injection (parameterise queries, escape output). A7: Auth Failures (bcrypt, account lockout, MFA). These three cover 60%+ of breaches.
HSTS: force HTTPS. CSP: control script/style sources. X-Frame-Options: DENY — prevent clickjacking. X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff. Set these in nginx/Spring Security/Express for every app.

project.brief: You are building a REST API for a social platform with posts and comments. Users can register, log in, post content, and comment. Design the security architecture: authentication method, session management, input handling, and security headers. Identify which OWASP Top 10 risks apply and how you mitigate each.

checklist.text: Authentication: use JWT with HttpOnly+SameSite cookie, 15 min access token + 7 day refresh token rotation
Password storage: bcrypt with cost 12. Never log passwords. Validate strength server-side.
Input: all DB queries use parameterised statements / JPA @Query. Post/comment content escaped before rendering.
Authorisation: verify user owns the resource server-side for every mutating request. No client trust.
Headers: HSTS, CSP, X-Frame-Options: DENY, X-Content-Type-Options: nosniff configured in Spring Security.

hints: Spring Security default CSRF protection works for form-based apps. For JWT APIs, disable CSRF (stateless = no session cookies to forge), add JWT filter instead.
Check OWASP Testing Guide for each feature: https://owasp.org/www-project-testing-guide
Swagger/OpenAPI: mark all endpoints with security schemes. Unauthenticated = 401, Unauthorised = 403.
